nativeRange.habitat: Tends to be found over sandy and silty bottoms of coastal shallows, lagoons, and seaward reef margins from 1–100 m depth; often partially buried in soft substrate

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on small crustaceans, clams, polychaete worms, and small bottom-dwelling fishes in the wild. In aquaria, newly-introduced specimens tend to accept only live foods (live enriched adult brine shrimp, grass/glass shrimp, and black worms); many individuals have been reported to transition over time to frozen mysis, chopped shrimp, and other meaty foods offered via feeding stick. Considered a notable starvation risk if it refuses to eat.

goodWith: peaceful invertebrates too large to be eaten
avoidWith: small bottom-dwelling fish that fit in mouth
tankmates that monopolize food at feeding time

summary: The Flying Gurnard (Dactyloptena orientalis) is an Indo-Pacific bottom-dweller famous for unfurling its enormous, fan-like blue-spotted pectoral fins. Generally regarded as an advanced-care species; tends to be shy, easily out-competed at feeding, and often refuses prepared foods on arrival.
careNotes:
The Flying Gurnard is a benthic Indo-Pacific species that uses thickened, finger-like anterior pelvic rays to 'walk' across sandy substrates. The spectacular fan-like pectoral fins, normally folded against the body, are flared as a startle display and reveal iridescent blue spots along the trailing edge. Wild specimens occur from the Red Sea and East Africa east to Hawaii and the Marquesas, north to southern Japan, and south to Australia and New Zealand, generally over sand and rubble in lagoons and on outer-reef slopes from 1–100 m.

Tank setup: Sources generally advise a large footprint with extensive open sand and minimal rockwork — the elongated pectoral fins are delicate and can be damaged on sharp aquascape. Sand should be soft and at least a few centimetres deep; specimens have been observed partially burying themselves when stressed. Low water flow is generally recommended.

Feeding: Newly-imported specimens are frequently reluctant feeders. Most sources agree that live foods (enriched adult brine shrimp, grass shrimp, black worms) are needed initially, with gradual transition to mysis and other meaty frozen foods via target-feeding. Tankmates that out-compete for food should be avoided, as gurnards are slow-moving and easily out-paced at feeding time.

Reef compatibility: Generally considered not safe with small ornamental shrimp or small bottom-dwelling fish, both of which it tends to prey upon. Sessile corals are typically left undisturbed, but the fish requires a sand-dominated layout that is at odds with a dense reefscape.

Unique features: Dactylopterids are capable of producing audible grunts using their swim-bladder musculature, and the digitate anterior pelvic-fin rays are used to probe the substrate for prey — both traits are unusual among aquarium fishes.

Cross-source disagreements (informational):
- Adult size: LiveAquaria states ~14 in (35.5 cm); QualityMarine ~15 in (38 cm); FishBase 40 cm TL (common 20 cm); Reeflings 40 cm. Range 20–40 cm reflects this spread.
- Minimum tank: LiveAquaria 180 gal (~681 L); QualityMarine 200 gal (~757 L); Reeflings 200 L (53 gal). The 200 L figure is an outlier — Reeflings rates the species 'moderate/easy' overall, but the consensus among hobby retailers and the prose ('large unobstructed swimming area', 'spacious', 'large open areas') supports a larger footprint. Settled on 700 L per playbook nice-values list, cautious side.
- Care level: LiveAquaria 'Expert Only'; QualityMarine 'Moderate'; Reeflings 'Moderate to easy'. Middle value selected ('advanced'), reflecting that the well-documented feeding-transition difficulty drives the overall difficulty rather than basic husbandry.
- Temperament: LiveAquaria 'peaceful'; QualityMarine and Reeflings note 'solitary, can be territorial' but also 'shy, easily picked on'. Settled on 'peaceful' since the shy/timid description is the more consistent behavioural picture; conspecifics should still be kept apart.
- Reef safe: LiveAquaria 'No'; QualityMarine page-level 'Yes' but prose notes predation on small fish/shrimp. Settled on 'no' per the predation evidence.
breedingNotes: Reproduction in this species is documented as pelagic broadcast spawning; eggs and larvae are planktonic and well outside the scope of typical home aquaria. Captive breeding has not been reported in the aquarium literature.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for hobby-care parameters (tank min 180 gal, temp 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, care 'Expert Only', diet, reef-safe 'No'). Collection-page URL substituted with the resolved product page.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Dactylopteridae; Cuvier, 1829), distribution (Red Sea to Marquesas, southern Japan to New Zealand), max size 40 cm TL (common 20 cm), depth 1–100 m, preferred temperature 23.3–29°C, diet (crustaceans, clams, small fish).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Confirmed ~15 in adult size, minimum 200 gal, temp 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12. Care 'Moderate'. Noted feeding difficulty, requirement for limited rockwork, and warning about poisonous/sharp spines.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Confirmed 40 cm adult, temp 22–26°C, pH 8.0–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, solitary/territorial, walks on pelvic-ray finger-like extensions. Tank minimum listed as 200 L — notably smaller than LiveAquaria (180 gal) and QualityMarine (200 gal); treated as outlier per playbook tank-size rule (cautious-larger).

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawner with planktonic larvae; not known to have been bred in home aquaria.
